title: Joy
subtitle: Cultivating Appreciative Gladness Without Indulgence
epigraph: Oh, how happily we live without hate among those with hate, among people who hate we live without hate.
paragraphs: Being spring—or April anyway—I thought we would have a go at joy.

The first thing I did was look up all the synonyms we have: pleasure, enjoyment, happiness, gladness, delight, thrill, exaltation, elation, bliss, ecstasy, rapture, and absorption. That's not bad, is it? And it's all good. It's not as though the Buddha asked us to be miserable all the time, even though most of the teaching is about getting rid of suffering.

The Buddha was once asked how he feels when he teaches and somebody doesn't accept the teaching. He said he feels all right with it—it's calmer. But when somebody does accept his teaching, he feels happy, feels joyful.

Now, it's an interesting thing that none of my teachers ever introduced us to the practice of joy. Very strange. Mettā, mettā all the time, compassion a little bit, but joy never. I think the reason was their fear of indulgence. And that's what goes wrong, of course. All these beautiful mental states to which we are heir corrupt as soon as we try to hold on to them or want more of them.

So it's a case of recognising that nothing is repeatable—when it's gone, it's gone, and there's no point hankering after it. If you take a morsel of food which you delight in, once you've swallowed it and the aftertaste is gone, that's it. Let go.
This fear of indulgence actually destroys joy. And I noticed it in the monastic life more than in lay people.

At the monastery, Kanduboda, there would obviously be occasions when people came with special foods to celebrate a birthday, a marriage, and so on. One of the things that is quite lovely is buffalo curd, miikiri. It was handed out, and you put loads of sugar with it, or kitul juice—a bit like maple juice—and it's wonderful.

We're all eating it, and there was a monk sat next to me who was the picture of the ascetic—very gaunt, cheeks sunken in. I knew him. As we got round to the curd, while we were eating, he was glaring at it. And as we came to the finish, he leant forward quite slowly, lifted it up slowly, and then basically shovelled it down his throat. I've always presumed he was just so afraid of indulgence. That's no good, is it?

There was another case too. My teacher, Sayadaw U Janaka, about ten years ago taught at a place called Beatenberg, near Interlaken, right by those beautiful lakes in Switzerland. From there, on a beautiful day, you can see three great mountains: the Eiger, the Mönch, and the Jungfrau.

During the retreat the weather was miserable—rainy, cloudy, and so on. Then one day it all opened up: a glorious sight, the white peaks against a deep blue sky. Everybody on the retreat went out onto the balcony and outside to have a look. Well, he gave them hell, didn't he? He upset a lot of people. Told them they were useless meditators, were just indulging, that he was wasting his time, and all sorts. I don't think he was invited again.

I remember whenever I would say something like, "Isn't that a beautiful flower?" he would say, "Ah yes, but it fades. It fades, it dies." A bit of a killjoy really. But his intention, of course, was to awaken me to the problem of indulgence.
And that seed of indulgence is always there, right to the bitter end. Mara is always right there, just waiting for the moment to creep up on us.

So it's a case of recognising these two things. If we begin to see the consequences of indulgence, we might make ourselves afraid to enjoy anything at all. But actually, consider this: you have a biscuit, you finish it, the last bit goes down, the flavour's gone—and then comes the thought, 'I think I'll have another one.' As soon as that arises, well, that's greed. So you have to sit there and wait for it to exhaust itself.
Once you've done that, you've exhausted the karmic consequence of the greed that arose while you were eating that biscuit. I think it's as simple as that.

I know somebody who got into the habit of eating biscuits just before bed, and he was getting rather worried about it. So instead, he turned to music. Now that's a skilful thing to do. If you find that an indulgence feels a bit overwhelming, put your attention on some other joyful thing that you like to do.

Now the question is, why do we mostly remember the painful parts of the day and hardly any of the joys? Well, I suppose the reason is that painful things hurt. But unfortunately, this does shadow out the joys.
So we have to make a special effort to remember the joys of the day. Instead of obsessing about our failures and upsets, we can contemplate our successes and joys.

The interesting thing is, the more you make yourself aware of the joys of the day, the more you bend that way—and the more you see what is actually undermining your joy. This, of course, creates even greater joy. So we try to rid ourselves of behaviours that undermine our joy, and over time our joy grows.
And of course, this is done with a certain affectionate awareness towards ourselves—being kind to ourselves, gentle to ourselves, seeking what lifts the heart. Music, nature, friendship, and so on.

Now, this word mudita, which is one of the four Brahma-viharas—remember, love, compassion, joy, and peacefulness or equanimity—these are the four great mental states, and we can develop them indefinitely. Mudita is often translated as appreciative joy or reciprocal joy. What it does, of course, is undermine envy and jealousy.
I define those two this way: envy is wanting what the other person has, and jealousy is wanting what the other person has and hating them for having it in the first place.

It's interesting: in the East, if you make a donation to a cause—say, to a monastery—all the names are posted up with exactly how much you gave. So it starts off with, I don't know, maybe a thousand pounds and goes all the way down to a couple of shillings. That's an old word, isn't it?

This is done because in the East it's understood that people will rejoice in the good karma you've gained by your offering. Now when it came to Bill Gates giving his millions away, I wonder what our response was. 'Well, he got all that money, he should be able to give something away—the one percenters.' Did we say, 'Oh, good on Bill, may he receive many blessings'? 'He's got enough blessings.'
I think it comes as a revelation that we can actually be more joyful for others than for ourselves. This isn't strange, of course, to parents who rejoice in the successes of their offspring.

I remember once—I must have been about twelve, because my sister's eleven years younger than me—I got back from school and sat opposite my mother. She held Maria and encouraged her to walk towards me. And of course when she did, it was joy. It was wonderful to see this little toddler trying to make her way.

Joy is also restorative—somebody's called it 'restorative joy.' There you are, down in the dumps, and you think to yourself, how can I lift my heart? How can I bring joy into it? It doesn't always have to be amazing ecstatic joy—just feeling happy, generally happy. That's when you meet up with a friend, or watch a good film, and so on.

In the monastic life, of course, the daily routine tends to be the same. Even when there's a feast day, the only difference is you turn up for a better meal and then go back to the usual. So it can move you towards a sense of apathy, a sense of laziness. And it was then that I realised the amazing power of a long, mindful cup of tea. No doubt about it.
Now the ethical joys are really what the Buddha points to. As far as he's concerned, virtue, goodness, equals happiness.
In chapter twenty of the Dhammapada, where many of the verses are devoted to being happy, this is what he says:
Oh, how happily we live without hate among those with hate. Among people who hate, we live without hate.
Oh, how happily we live without misery among those in misery. Among people in misery, we live without misery.
Oh, how happily we live without ambition among those with ambition. Among people with ambition, we live without ambition.

Just a note on that ambition: there's nothing wrong with offering our talents for the benefit of the company, or people, and so on. But of course it becomes corrupt when we start thinking about money and power.

So what have we talked about? We've talked about the enemies of joy. We've seen how corruptive indulgence is, how it becomes an obsession—what they call feeding the dragon. Because of that, we can't allow ourselves to simply enjoy something. And then there's the indulgence itself, which becomes a trap. That's feeding the dragon.

So here are some exercises. You have to make a resolution. If at the end of today you sit and think, how happy was I today?—and all you can remember is the miseries—then you have to make a determination that tomorrow you'll actually acknowledge when you're happy. Any level of happiness, any level of joy. It could just be contentment. Just acknowledge it. And doing that, of course, you remember it by the end of the day.

The other thing you might try is to contemplate every evening for about a week—maybe five or ten minutes—and just go back in your life and think of all the joys you've had. All the joys. By the end of the week, you'll have a big smile on your face.
Another practice is the meditation on joy, which works like mettā. You go through all the categories, but the blessings are: may you be joyful, may you be ever more joyful.
Those are the ones regularly used. I like to add: may you attain the sublime peace of nibbāna.

The problem with nibbāna is that there are no emotions in nibbāna. So it's not that sort of happiness. In fact, that's what Sāriputta said: it's the very absence of emotions which is the bliss of nibbāna. That's a bit difficult for us, but at least we can make that intention—because according to the Buddha, this is the highest of all happinesses.
So the full blessing runs: may you be joyful, may you be ever more joyful, may you attain the sublime peace of nibbāna.
I think 'peace' rather than 'bliss'. I've sometimes used 'bliss', but 'peace' gives you, I think, a slightly better feel for it.
I can only hope my words have been of some assistance, that I haven't caused confusion, and that through your development of joy, you will be joyful sooner rather than later.
